II.3. The Ciompi “Revolt” On the morning of July 21, 1378 (different chroniclers and contemporaries detail different days, such as the 19 and 20 of July, in their writings), the Palazzo Vecchio had been densely packed with people. title: Revolt of the Ciompi | Peasant Uprising, Social Conflict, Florence
highlights: Revolt of the Ciompi, (1378), insurrection of the lower classes of Florence that briefly brought to power one of the most democratic governments in Florentine history. The ciompi (“wool carders”) were the most radical of the groups that revolted, and they were defeated by the more conservative elements in  ...    upon to take part in the revolt in late June, continued to agitate on their own during the month of July. They presented a series of petitions to the Signoria (executive council of Florence) demanding a more equitable fiscal policy and the right to establish guilds for those groups not already organized. Then, on July 22, the lower classes forcibly took over the government, placing one of their members, the wool carder Michele di Lando,  ...    implement all their demands. Conflicting interests of the minor guilds and the ciompi became evident. On August 31 a large group of the ciompi that had gathered in the Piazza della Signoria was easily

title: The Ciompi Uprising | The Florentine
highlights: When Florence’s lowest-paid textile workers seized power, and how betrayal and ambition brought their revolution to a  ...    Unrest had been fomenting in Florence, especially in the Santa Croce and Oltrarno neighbourhoods, before it broke out into what started, at least in the beginning, as a bloodless coup on June 24, 1378, which was led by the humblest of workers in the cloth industry, including day labourers, wool combers, dyers and those doing poorly paid jobs like the many shop boys employed in the trade. Known as the Ciompi, deriving from the French word compère (meaning “buddy” or  ...    The revolt was sparked by the election of Salvestro de’ Medici, a cousin of the banking branch of the family, who was nominated gonfalonier by popular acclaim on June 18, 1378. With the complicity of the Franciscan friars of Santa Croce, the Ciompi flooded into the Palazzo Vecchio carrying the banner of the Angel Gabriel seeking to protect their meagre wages and demand the minimum civil

title: Ciompi-Aufstand
highlights: Sommermonaten des Jahres 1378. Der Aufstand begann im Juni und dauerte bis Ende August an. In ihm spielten die Ciompi, die Florentiner Arbeiter in der Kleidungsindustrie, eine  ...    Die Angehörigen des popolo minuto hatten keine Bürgerrechte und konnten sich nicht in einer  ...    Die Ciompi waren als ungelernte Arbeiter für das Waschen, Kämmen und Fetten der Wolle zuständig. Ciampo war  ...    Ein typischer Streit unter den Fraktionen innerhalb der Grandi entzündete den Aufstand. Mitglieder der unteren Schichten nahmen im Juli ihr Schicksal in die eigene Hand; sie brachten eine Reihe von Petitionen vor die Signoria, die städtische Regierung, in denen sie eine gerechtere Steuerpolitik forderten sowie das wertvolle Recht, sich selbst in Gilden organisieren zu dürfen. Am 22.Juli übernahmen sie gewaltsam die Regierung, setzten den Wollkämmer Michele di Lando als Gonfaloniere ein und hissten ihr Banner auf dem Palazzo della Signoria.
